subtopic: India's Forex Reserves and RBI's Financial Framework
title: India's Forex Reserves
focus: The composition of India's foreign exchange reserves, the rationale behind the increasing share of gold, associated risks, and the key components of the reserves.
rationale: This section details a crucial aspect of India's external sector management. It covers the strategic shift towards gold in forex reserves, its implications (diversification, risk mitigation), and the fundamental components of forex reserves (FCA, Gold, SDR, RTP), all of which are frequently tested concepts in UPSC Prelims.
sectionExcerpt: According to the RBI, India's gold reserves have increased, taking the share of gold in the foreign reserves to nearly 15% from around 7% a decade ago. The RBI is increasing gold reserves for diversification (de-dollarization), risk mitigation against currency volatility, and as a hedge against inflation. However, holding gold involves risks like reduced liquidity, zero yield, and high storage costs. The main components of India's Foreign Exchange Reserves are Foreign Currency Assets (FCA), Gold Reserves, Special Drawing Rights (SDR), and the Reserve Tranche Position (RTP) with the IMF.
evidenceBullets: The share of gold in India's foreign reserves has increased to nearly 15%.
Reasons for increasing gold reserves include diversification, risk mitigation against currency volatility, and hedging against inflation.
Risks associated with storing gold include reduced liquidity, zero yield, and storage/security costs.
The four components of Foreign Exchange Reserves are Foreign Currency Assets (FCA), Gold Reserves, Special Drawing Rights (SDR), and Reserve Tranche Position (RTP).

subtopic: India's Forex Reserves and RBI's Financial Framework
title: Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021 (RB-IOS)
focus: The recent expansion of the Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021 to include Central and State Co-operative Banks, and the scheme's primary objective.
rationale: This section covers a specific regulatory update regarding a key consumer protection mechanism in the banking sector. The inclusion of co-operative banks under the RB-IOS is a significant development and a potential question area for UPSC, testing awareness of recent changes in financial regulation.
sectionExcerpt: The Central and State Co-operative Banks have been brought under the ambit of the Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021 (RB-IOS, 2021) under the Banking Regulation Act, 1949. The scheme's objective is to provide customers of regulated entities a speedy, cost-effective, and expeditious alternate grievance redress mechanism.
evidenceBullets: Central and State Co-operative Banks have been brought under the ambit of the Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021.
This was done under the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
The scheme aims to provide a speedy, cost-effective, and expeditious alternate grievance redress mechanism for customers.
